title: From 'Prompt and Pray' to Production: My Journey Beyond Vibe Coding
author: Bob Glithero Bob Glithero Published May 8
text:
From 'Prompt and Pray' to Production: My Journey Beyond Vibe Coding
"Coding? Nah, I Just Vibe Now.”
That’s a line people throw around, often tongue-in-cheek, when they talk about vibe coding—a new way of building software by describing what you want to an AI and letting it generate the code. To outsiders, it sounds like magic. To engineers, it sounds like madness. But as someone who built a working application, I can tell you: it’s neither.
Vibe coding typically means using AI to create code without pausing to review – just run the code and see if the output “feels” right. But I prefer to call what I did, “chat coding.” The difference is a process that involves thoughtful design, carefully sequenced tasks, and plenty of review time for both code and outputs.
That’s not to say pure vibe coding is useless. It’s fine for quick scripts, validating ideas, and small personal projects. But chat coding doesn’t ignore good engineering practices. When paired with clear specifications, thoughtful data modeling, modular design, and a healthy dose of review, chat coding transforms into more than just "prompt and pray." It becomes a surprisingly effective way to turn product ideas into working software—fast.
In this post, I’ll share what chat coding really looks like in practice, and why I think it’s a tool worth understanding for hobbyist and other semi-technical developers. I’ll even give you several tips you can use to make your chat coding journey easier!
First, I came to this project with a use case already in mind.
The Evolution of Search: Why User Intent Trumps Keywords
Generative search engines like Google's AI Overviews (AIO) use semantic similarity matching and other techniques to answer user questions directly with content scraped from publishers’ websites. Only content that AIO cites for validation of its answers is promoted to the user. By diverting traffic away from source websites, AI search shifts value away from website owners towards the search engine itself.
So reducing organic traffic leakage isn't just about having the right keywords or page structure anymore. It's about having content that truly matches what the user is looking for at a conceptual level.
Elastic has a great example of the difference between keywords and semantics: "milk chocolate" versus "chocolate milk." The words are identical, but the order completely changes the meaning. Modern search engines get this distinction. They're not just matching words anymore - they're analyzing concepts.
You need to shrink the gap between what your content says and what users are searching for. You might be using all the right words but missing the underlying concepts that connect to user intent.
An Application to Connect Concepts with User Intent
As a product marketer, I’m obsessed with writing content that connects with customers. So my goal was to build an application for content analysis that shows opportunities to make better connections between the concepts in my content and what users are searching for. It does this by finding opportunities to amplify the semantic signals in my content.
At its core, the app uses embeddings to convert text (words, phrases, or documents) into numerical vectors in a high-dimensional space. Think of an embedding model like a map-making algorithm, but instead of working with flat 2-D maps, it lays out embeddings oriented in a many-dimensional mathematical space. Similar texts, synonyms, and paraphrases have vectors that lie close together in space, indicating whether they have related meanings.
Semantic similarity matching is nothing new, but I wanted to package it in a way that’s easy for a marketing team to use.
Among other things, I can use this app to:
There are limitations, of course. Google and other AI-search engines combine several models, not just use one, and they have user behavior data that no one else has. So this app can only ever be an approximation to their content matching algorithms. But to my eye it works surprisingly well, and even when I don’t agree with the scoring, the app isn’t wildly off – I can review the text and understand why the scoring went the way it did.
Tips for Success with Chat Coding
Don’t let this happen to you (but at least the story had a happy ending):
Start with a functional specification
Think about the user and what their goals are. What does the user journey look like? What inputs they will need to provide, what actions they need to take, and what responses the application will provide in return?
Starting with at least a basic functional specification provides several benefits:
If you’re working on a multi-user system, or you’ll work with sensitive data, then you’ll also need to consider elements like security, encryption, and access control.
Understand the idiosyncrasies of your coding assistant
Language models all have distinctive patterns, tendencies, and limitations that can affect your project's success. Each model has its own "personality" based on its architecture and training data that affects how it interprets your prompts.
Recommended by LinkedIn
I use Anthropic AI Research Claude 3.7 because I like its coding style and the way its Projects folder works. However, I prefer OpenAI ChatGPT’s style for explaining unfamiliar software libraries and code. I've also found that when Claude got stuck on a problem, Google Gemini could suggest good alternatives, making a multi-model strategy quite effective for overcoming individual limitations.
It wasn't all puppies and ice cream, though. Claude has a tendency to over-engineer solutions. I once asked it to design a simple blogging website for my personal use. Claude delivered that and more, even providing Terraform scripts for an enterprise-scale cloud deployment. Very impressive, but way beyond what I asked for. So I found myself constantly reining in Claude’s output to stop it wandering off.
Knowing these tendencies helps you select the right model for your specific project requirements. As you become more familiar with your model’s tendencies, you’ll learn to prompt it more efficiently.
Watch out for obsolete training data
LLMs have a cut off for training data, but APIs evolve all the time. If the model’s training data is obsolete, it will introduce bugs in your code that can be hard to find and remove.
In my project, I used Streamlit , a Python framework for web application development. The problem was that Streamlit’s APIs evolved past the model’s training data, which relied on outdated experimental features. It would routinely inject regressions into my code unless I explicitly gave it the most up-to-date documentation. So for the fastest path forward, start by giving your model fresh documentation and code samples.
Use disposable scripts to validate ideas
One of the genius things about chat coding is the ability to quickly generate throwaway scripts. You can quickly validate your approaches to solving issues without investing a lot of time.
For complex coding tasks try getting the model to write a simpler version first, check that it works and then iterate from there. You can even seed the model with code examples or tutorial notebooks to guide it.
Remember that the goal isn't just to generate code quickly but also to use these scripts as learning tools for a deeper understanding of the problem you’re solving. As you validate each component of your solution, you build confidence in your approach while minimizing the risk of major design flaws in development.
Be as modular as possible
Even with the best initial structure, AI-generated code often needs refinement. Designing for modularity and separation of responsibilities makes it easier to contain problems to a single module. It’s a lot easier to develop your app if you can test and refine each component before integrating them.
I needed to change my data model twice. Pinecone Serverless (my vector database), had introduced a list function that made sorting and filtering easier. I refined my record ID structure to take advantage of it. I also decided to capture new metadata for tagging the records. So I updated the data model, and carefully propagated changes through the rest of my application, component by component, starting with my data management module and then working up the rest of the app.
If had built a big ball of spaghetti code instead, untangling it would have been messy and tedious.
Use artifacts to ground the model’s output
This tip is especially important. By systematically creating and leveraging artifacts throughout your development process, you establish clear ground truth that AI models can use to generate more accurate, contextually appropriate code:
Function typing: Type annotations create explicit documentation that shows the contract between components: what inputs are expected; what outputs are guaranteed. When you need to refactor code with AI help, type hints provide crucial context about how different components interact.
Data schema and sample records: Well-documented data classes define the shape of your data and provide contextual information that helps the AI understand the system's structure, relationships, and data flow within your application. Including samples of expected input/output data provides additional training examples that leads to more accurate processing and error-handling code.
Error logs: Log messages provide visibility into your application’s runtime behavior. If an operation fails, these logs become concrete evidence that will help the model diagnose where the issue is happening. Showing the model sample output records, together with error logs, is a powerful way to speed up debugging.
If there’s a problem, the error trace will pinpoint it quickly.
I also added helper methods to generate intermediate calculations that indicated if my processing logic was off. Seeing the intermediate results, the error logs, and the data records helped me to zero in on problems quickly. I could also hand these to the model, and it would nearly always generate the correct code on the next prompt. It made development go a lot faster!
My Code Review with Claude
At the end, I asked Claude to assess the overall soundness of the system design and implementation:
“In summary, the codebase demonstrates solid software engineering practices with a well-defined architecture and good separation of concerns. The main opportunities for improvement are in implementing caching of embeddings to enhance resource efficiency.”
Conclusion: The Rise of the Semi-Technical Builder
I think of chat coding as occupying a new market space that lies between professional coding tools or the workflow needs of experienced developers, and low-code tools, which offer ease of use at the expense of expressiveness. It’s true that you can’t approach chat-coding without at least some amount of software development knowledge. But I do think that the bar is far lower than the years it takes to acquire the knowledge and experience of a professional developer.
There’s a large addressable market of sufficiently-skilled professionals who could be successful at chat coding: SQL developers, BI dashboard developers, and MOOC course graduates who have acquired basic language skills. I don’t have broad Python experience myself, but from my past work creating demos and notebooks I know the basics of moving and transforming data and managing machines. That was more than enough background to get started.
Overall, I’m very pleased with how this project went. It wasn't just about building an app, it was also about pushing the limits of what I thought was possible from this style of development. If you’ve ever wondered if you could easily create well-designed applications tailored to your specific workflow, give chat coding a try!
Know someone who's trying to get real value from AI? Tag them with this article, then follow me for more insights!
Update: AI search is siphoning your organic traffic, but I found a way to fight back. Using my application to optimize the content, I got Google's AI to cite this article as a top source—even in a competitive field. Next: here's how marketers can adapt and thrive in the zero-click era.
